does: This paper develops an AI pipeline to automate hospital quality improvement factor discovery, the process of identifying modifiable contributing factors to quality issues. Rather than assuming the task is well-defined, the authors formalize QI factor discovery as a learning problem where both LLM prompts and natural-language task specifications are learned iteratively. The pipeline combines classical AI/ML development steps with exploratory sense-making, using data from actual hospital QI initiatives. The approach moves beyond traditional expert-driven tools like fishbone diagrams and chart reviews by scaling discovery across multiple cases while maintaining auditability and reproducibility.
connects: This work bridges two distinct research traditions: hospital quality improvement methodology (fishbone diagrams, Lean Healthcare, chart reviews) and AI alignment challenges in deploying language models to fuzzy real-world tasks. It builds on recent work in prompt engineering and AI specification learning, extending alignment methods beyond well-defined tasks to exploratory domains. The paper responds to the limitation that current AI methods assume crisp problem definitions, whereas QI factor discovery is iterative and relies on tacit expert knowledge. It also connects to research on formalizing qualitative processes and incorporating domain expertise into AI systems, particularly in high-stakes healthcare settings where reproducibility and auditing are essential.
enables: This work opens pathways for scaling QI processes across hospital systems by automating the expensive discovery phase while maintaining human expert oversight. The framework for learning natural-language specifications alongside prompts could generalize to other exploratory, fuzzy domains requiring domain expertise—clinical decision-making, safety incident analysis, or organizational process improvement beyond healthcare. The pipeline potentially enables hospitals to more systematically identify and implement improvements, accelerating the feedback loop between problem identification and intervention. It also points toward methods for making implicit expert judgment more transparent and auditable in high-stakes AI applications, and could facilitate knowledge transfer between hospitals facing similar quality challenges.
